ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a guacamole entry (FOODON_03302114) but does not have a combined guacamole-with-tortilla-chips composite dish entry; closest_parent used for the guacamole component. Tortilla chips are a separate item.
ontology.foodex2_description: Avocado-based dip (guacamole) — closest FoodEx2 category for avocado-based prepared dip
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated guacamole-with-chips composite code. A00LC (avocado products) used as closest defensible parent for the guacamole component.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical concept exists for guacamole with tortilla chips as a food item.

serving.description: 1 serving with chips (approximately 85 g guacamole + 28 g chips)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of guacamole with tortilla chips consists of approximately 85 g of fresh guacamole alongside 28 g (about one ounce) of tortilla chips, totalling roughly 113 g per serving.

nutrient_source.title: Guacamole, ready-to-eat (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #09038 avocado base) combined with Tortilla chips, plain (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy #19056); composite calculated at 75% guacamole / 25% chips by weight
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA entry exists for the composite dish 'guacamole with tortilla chips'. Values are calculated as a weighted composite: 75 g guacamole per 100 g total (based on USDA FDC #167746 'Guacamole, ready-to-eat') and 25 g tortilla chips per 100 g total (based on USDA FDC SR Legacy #19056 'Snacks, tortilla chips, plain'). Energy and macros are blended proportionally. This reflects a typical restaurant-style serving ratio.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. Tortilla chips GI ~42 (corn-based); avocado/guacamole GI effectively low (~15). Composite GI estimated at 40 for the combined dish.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed journal (Diabetes Care)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for the composite dish 'guacamole with tortilla chips'. GI estimated as weighted composite: tortilla chips (plain, corn) GI ~42 per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008; avocado/guacamole GI ~15 (very low carbohydrate, high fat food). Weighted at 75% guacamole / 25% chips by weight yields estimated composite GI of approximately 40. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (40 × 21.9) / 100 ≈ 8.8, where carbohydrate per 113 g serving = 19.4 × 1.13 = 21.9 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from tortilla chips and added salt in guacamole
hypertension.guidance: A single restaurant serving of guacamole with chips contributes approximately 246 mg sodium; individuals with hypertension should monitor portion size and request reduced-salt preparations, as sodium intake above 2,300 mg/day is associated with elevated blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate load from tortilla chips with moderate glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: The tortilla chip component contributes a moderate glycemic load (GL ~8.8 per serving); individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit chip portions and pair with the avocado-rich guacamole, whose monounsaturated fat and fibre content help attenuate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High monounsaturated fat from avocado with low saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Guacamole is rich in monounsaturated fatty acids (primarily oleic acid from avocado), which are associated with improved LDL-to-HDL cholesterol ratios; however, the tortilla chip component adds refined carbohydrates and sodium, so portion control is advised for individuals with cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Guacamole with tortilla chips is a popular Mexican-origin snack and appetiser consisting of fresh guacamole — a creamy dip made primarily from mashed ripe avocado seasoned with lime juice, salt, onion, cilantro, and optionally jalapeño or tomato — served alongside crispy corn tortilla chips. The guacamole is smooth to chunky in texture depending on preparation style, with a rich, buttery flavour from the avocado balanced by the acidity of lime and the heat of chilli. Tortilla chips provide a salty, crunchy contrast. Nutritionally, the dish is notable for its high monounsaturated fat content from avocado, which supports cardiovascular health, and its dietary fibre content (approximately 3.8 g per 100 g composite), which aids satiety and glycaemic control. The dish is naturally gluten-free, vegan, and dairy-free. The glycemic index of the composite is estimated at approximately 40 (low), driven primarily by the tortilla chip component, while the avocado base contributes negligible glycaemic load. Clinically, the sodium content from chips and added salt warrants attention for hypertensive individuals, and the carbohydrate portion from chips should be moderated for those managing Type 2 Diabetes. In Singapore, this dish is served at Mexican restaurants such as Three Amigos and Fiesta Mexico, typically priced around SGD 13 per serving.
common_user_queries: How many calories in guacamole with tortilla chips?
Can I eat guacamole with chips if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of tortilla chips?
How much fat is in guacamole?
Is avocado dip with chips high in sodium?
Where can I get guacamole in Singapore?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for the composite dish 'guacamole with tortilla chips'. Nutrient values are calculated as a weighted composite from two USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries (guacamole ready-to-eat and plain tortilla chips) at a 75:25 ratio by weight, which is a reasonable restaurant-serving approximation. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are a weighted composite estimate (75% guacamole, 25% tortilla chips by weight) derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries #167746 and #19056 respectively. A direct approved-source entry for the composite dish does not exist. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy data for both components; tortilla chips and avocado-based guacamole contain no industrially produced trans fats. 3. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; avocado and corn tortilla chips are plant-based foods containing no dietary cholesterol. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero; this is an entirely plant-based food with no animal-derived ingredients. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — naturally zero; neither avocado nor corn tortilla chips are significant sources of vitamin D. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all estimated from the same USDA composite calculation and are provided as best-effort values. 7. GI is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 8. This food is Mexican cuisine; multilingual naming rules for Singaporean dishes do not apply. No mandatory non-English local-language names are required for Mexican cuisine under Project Veritas v2 rules. The Spanish romanized form 'Guacamole con totopos' is included as a culturally relevant alias. 9. FoodOn URI FOODON_03302114 is used as closest_parent for the guacamole component only; no composite dish code exists in FoodOn. FoodEx2 code A00LC is used as closest_parent for avocado-based products.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected food_name from all-caps 'GUACAMOLE WITH CHIPS' to canonical title case 'Guacamole with Tortilla Chips'.
Computed composite nutrient values from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries at 75:25 guacamole-to-chips ratio.
Estimated GI at 40 using University of Sydney GI Database data for tortilla chips and avocado, with analogous estimate documented.
Added zero-value justifications in notes for trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, and vitamin_d_mcg.
Confirmed Mexican cuisine does not require mandatory non-English local-language names under G6; Spanish romanized alias included.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases are fully consistent.
Set ontology match types to 'closest_parent' or 'none' only — no invented codes.
Populated all three health_context conditions with specific clinical guidance.
